subject: English

good_contexts: Student survey statistics (e.g., screen time, sports participation, reading habits)
misconceptions_to_address: Making inferences not directly supported by the text
Defining vocabulary out of the passage's specific context
Reliance on external general knowledge rather than passage content
diagram_or_asset_needs: Simple diagrams illustrating a concept from the passage
avoid: Ambiguous or contradictory data within the passage
Questions with multiple equally correct interpretations
Topics that are overly sensitive or controversial for this age group
chapters.chapter_question_direction: Dear students, hone your reading and analytical skills! You'll be presented with an unseen factual passage, often with visual data. Read carefully to understand the main ideas, interpret the information, and answer questions based on the text and visuals provided. Pay close attention to details and context.

sample_question_directions.direction: Based on the passage, identify the most accurate statement regarding [specific data point/theme].
From the passage, state one reason for [observed trend].
Analyze the chart provided and explain how it supports/contradicts a point made in the passage about [topic].
Infer from the passage why [character/group] might feel [emotion/have opinion] about [topic].

core_concepts: Tense shifts in reported speech (e.g., simple past to past perfect)
Changes in time and place adverbs
Reporting verbs (said, told, asked, requested, ordered, etc.)
Converting commands/requests, statements, and questions into reported speech
skills_to_test: Applying rules of reported speech in contextual sentences
Identifying and correcting errors in reported speech constructions
good_contexts: Dialogues between students, teachers, friends, or family
misconceptions_to_address: Incorrect tense shifts (e.g., not shifting tenses when needed)
Failure to change pronouns or time/place adverbs correctly
Confusing the correct reporting verb for different sentence types (e.g., 'said' vs. 'asked')
Incorrect punctuation when dealing with direct speech
Mixing up rules for statements, questions, and commands when reporting
diagram_or_asset_needs: Simple dialogue bubbles or character outlines to represent speakers in contextual questions (optional)
avoid: Ambiguous direct speech that can be interpreted in multiple ways
Slang or highly informal language that is difficult to report formally
chapters.chapter_question_direction: Hello young grammarians! This section will test your understanding of Reported Speech. You'll convert direct speech into indirect speech, and vice-versa, making sure to correctly change tenses, pronouns, and time expressions. Focus on accurate transformation in realistic scenarios.

sample_question_directions.direction: Choose the correct reported speech for: '[Direct Speech Sentence]'.
Convert the following direct speech into indirect speech: 'He said, 'Please close the door.''
Complete the dialogue by reporting the given sentences correctly.
Identify and correct the error in the reported speech sentence: '[Incorrect Reported Speech Sentence]'.

chapters.chapter_title: Creative Writing Skills — Letter to the Editor
core_concepts: Formal letter format (sender/receiver addresses, date, subject, salutation, body, complimentary close)
Clearly stating a social or civic issue
Maintaining a formal and respectful tone
Understanding audience and purpose in formal communication
skills_to_test: Expressing opinions clearly, concisely, and persuasively
Adhering to prescribed formats and word limits
Using appropriate vocabulary for formal communication
good_contexts: Addressing local civic issues (e.g., sanitation, road safety, traffic congestion)
Promoting social causes (e.g., literacy, health awareness, environmental protection)
Suggesting improvements to community services or public facilities
Expressing concerns about school-related issues (e.g., bullying, extracurricular activities)
misconceptions_to_address: Incorrect or incomplete formal letter format
Rambling or repetitive content in the body paragraphs
Failure to include proper addresses, salutations, or complimentary closes
diagram_or_asset_needs: No specific diagrams; explanations may include a visual outline of letter format or model responses
avoid: Topics requiring specialized or adult professional knowledge
Prompts that do not clearly define the target audience, purpose, format, or word limit
chapters.chapter_question_direction: Budding writers, get ready to express yourselves formally! This section challenges you to write a 'Letter to the Editor' on various social issues. Remember to follow the correct formal letter format, maintain a polite and persuasive tone, and clearly present your views and solutions within the given word limit. Your ability to communicate effectively in a formal setting is key!

sample_question_directions.direction: Write a letter to the Editor of a national daily, expressing your concern about the rising problem of plastic waste in your city and suggesting ways to tackle it.
You are [Name], a resident of [Locality]. Write a letter to the Editor of a local newspaper highlighting the need for more public libraries in your area to encourage reading habits among children.

planning_notes: Generated subject direction plan for Class 9 English based on provided chapter sources and policies. All required fields populated per schema.
